title: Blood on Linen
timeEstimate: 10–30 minutes; cold-only protein, structured / vintage = pro
stainChemistry: Blood is protein with iron in it. Fresh blood is water-soluble in cold conditions and lifts easily. Heat or time turns the iron from red to brown and locks the protein into the linen — once that happens, the stain is much harder. Linen tolerates cold-water and cool-enzyme protocols (it's cellulose, similar to cotton) but is more delicate when wet — wrinkles aggressively and structured / lined / vintage / dry-clean-only linen warps under aggressive handling. The hard lines: NO hot water (sets protein) and NO alcohol on blood (sets protein). Chlorine bleach on white linen is bounded — only with care label permission and on solid white pieces with no elastane / colored trim.
whyThisWorks: Cold water keeps the protein from setting and rinses out the soluble portion. An enzyme pretreat in cool water (heat damages the enzymes AND the protein) breaks down residual protein. 3% peroxide on white or light linen handles the iron-brown shadow. Cool wash plus AIR-DRY flat (linen wrinkles aggressively when hung wet) finishes. Structured / vintage / dry-clean-only linen narrows the bench sharply — pro is the right call.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): blood is hemoglobin + plasma proteins + iron. Hemoglobin oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (red → brown) over hours. Linen is cellulose; tolerates cold liquid Protein Spotter (protease pretreat) plus NSD plus dilute peroxide on whites or sodium hydrosulfite at controlled dilution for set/oxidized residue. Structured pieces require pro because wet handling warps lining.
defaultAssumption: Cut, period stain, or kitchen mishap on a simple flat washable linen napkin / shirt / tablecloth, fresh and not yet hot-dried. Hot-dried = often permanent brown shadow. Structured / lined / vintage / dry-clean-only linen = pro from minute one.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Gloves on (biohazard). Dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen = pro from minute one. Simple flat washable linen = home candidate.
Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath. 60–90 seconds. Cold only — heat sets blood into linen permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus per label, COOL water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Dissolve enzyme pretreat in COOL water. Apply to spot, keep damp under a cloth, 15–20 min. Hot water above bath temperature damages enzymes AND sets the protein. Cool only.

spottingProtocol.technique: White or light linen only, hidden test on dyed
spottingProtocol.instruction: WHITE or very light linen only. Hidden-area test on dyed linen first. 3% peroxide damp cloth 5–10 min for residual iron-brown. Damp-cloth rinse. NEVER mix with bleach / ammonia / alkaline.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, cool water, drying rack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY FLAT — linen wrinkles aggressively when wet, hanging stretches the fiber. Inspect dry before any iron. NEVER hot dryer until cleared.

steps: 1. Care label triage. Gloves. Structured / vintage = pro.
3. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 15–20 min — never hot.
4. 3% H₂O₂ damp cloth for iron-brown on white / light linen, hidden test on dyed.
5. Cool wash + AIR-DRY flat. No heat until cleared.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile / Biz / Tide Plus / liquid Protein Spotter)
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool throughout — heat sets blood protein and iron-brown
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets protein permanently
NEVER alcohol on blood — sets the protein
Structured / lined / vintage linen = pro from minute one
Linen wrinkles aggressively wet — air-dry FLAT, not hanging
Hidden test on dyed linen before peroxide
Chlorine bleach on white linen — bounded, only with care label permission and no elastane / colored trim
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Is the Whole First Move
homeSolutions.instruction: Blood on linen is fixable IF you keep it cold. The recurring trap is hot water — locks the protein and turns the iron from red to brown permanently. Two hard rules: NO hot water (sets the stain) and NO alcohol on blood (sets the protein). Gloves on if it's not your blood — biohazard.

Dry-clean-only linen, structured pieces (lined jackets, drapery), or vintage linen all narrow the bench sharply — pro from minute one. Simple flat washable linen (napkin, plain shirt, basic tablecloth) = home candidate.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water Back-Flush from the BACK
homeSolutions.instruction: Turn the linen inside out. Hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water with a white towel underneath the front. Let cold water push the blood out the way it came in. 60 to 90 seconds. Cold only — even warm tap water sets the protein.

Dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Tide Plus) in COOL water per the label. Apply to the spot, keep it damp under a cloth, wait 15 to 20 minutes. Don't be tempted to use warm water 'to help' — heat above bath temperature damages the enzymes AND sets the protein. Cool only.

homeSolutions.agent: 3% Peroxide for Iron-Brown Shadow on White Linen
homeSolutions.instruction: WHITE or very light linen only: 3% drugstore peroxide on a damp cloth, press the spot 5 to 10 minutes, blot. Handles the iron-brown shadow that sometimes remains after the protein is gone. Hidden-area test on dyed linen first — skip if color transfers.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners — toxic fumes.

homeSolutions.instruction: Cool machine wash with normal detergent. AIR-DRY FLAT — linen wrinkles aggressively when wet, and hanging it stretches the fiber. Inspect dry before any iron. NEVER hot dryer until cleared. Heat at this step locks any residual protein or brown into the linen permanently.

steps: 1. Gloves on (biohazard). Structured / vintage linen = pro.
2. NO hot water. NO alcohol on blood.
3. Cold water back-flush 60–90 sec from the BACK.
4. Enzyme pretreat in COOL water, 15–20 min.
5. 3% peroxide damp cloth for iron-brown on white linen only, hidden test on dyed.
6. Cool wash + AIR-DRY FLAT. No iron until cleared.
products: Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Tide Plus)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop on dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen (pro). Stop if hot water or hot dryer was already used (often permanent brown shadow). Stop if hidden-area peroxide test fails on dyed linen. Stop after 2 home rounds without progress.
warnings: NEVER hot water — sets blood permanently
NEVER alcohol on blood — sets the protein
Structured / vintage linen = pro from minute one
Linen air-dries FLAT, not hanging — wrinkles aggressively
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline

neverDo: Never hot water on blood + linen
Never iron over a stained area before clearing
Never bare-handed if blood isn't your own
Never mix peroxide with bleach / ammonia / alkaline
fiberSensitivities: White or light washable linen: tolerates cool enzyme + 3% peroxide
Dyed linen: hidden test mandatory before peroxide
Structured / lined linen: pro — warps under wet handling
Vintage / antique linen: pro — fiber fragility under aggressive chemistry
Linen wrinkles aggressively wet — air-dry FLAT, not hanging

materialWarnings: No hot water — sets blood permanently
No alcohol on blood — sets the protein
Structured / vintage linen = pro from minute one
Iron-brown shadow needs separate peroxide pass on whites

escalation.when: Dry-clean-only / structured / lined / vintage linen. Hot water or hot dryer already used (often permanent). Alcohol pre-applied (often sets protein). 2 home rounds plateau. Hidden-area test fails on dyed linen.
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on [white / dyed / structured / vintage] linen, age [fresh / hours / hot-dried suspected], home-treated with [cold back-flush + cool enzyme pretreat + 3% peroxide on white + cool wash + air-dry flat]. No hot, no alcohol. [Residual brown / hot-dried suspected / structured piece]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with linen experience plus protein/iron-stain spotting capability

contextQuestions: Simple flat linen, structured / lined, or vintage?
Fresh, hours old, or hot-dried already?
Has alcohol been used on the spot?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Linen is more delicate than cotton when wet — wrinkles aggressively, structured pieces warp. Hot-dried blood leaves a permanent brown shadow most of the time. Alcohol pre-applied (rubbing alcohol or hand sanitizer to clean a cut) often sets the protein — disclose to the cleaner. The iron-brown shadow that remains after protein removal is a separate sub-stain — peroxide handles it on white linen, sodium hydrosulfite is the pro tool on dyed pieces.
